text:
RESTAGING BONE SCAN AND CT ABDOMEN/PELVIS

PATIENT: Raymond Kowalski
MRN: [REDACTED]
EXAMINATIONS: (1) Whole-body Technetium-99m methylene diphosphonate (Tc-99m MDP) planar bone scintigraphy with SPECT/CT of the pelvis and lumbar spine; (2) Contrast-enhanced CT of the abdomen and pelvis
CLINICAL INDICATION: 67-year-old male with metastatic castration-sensitive prostate cancer (Gleason 9, PSA 187 ng/mL at diagnosis, known osseous and pelvic nodal metastases) currently on androgen deprivation therapy (leuprolide) plus abiraterone acetate. Restaging approximately 6 months after initiation of systemic therapy to assess treatment response. Prior PSA 4.2 ng/mL at 3-month follow-up (97.8% decline from baseline). Comparison: Tc-99m bone scan and CT chest/abdomen/pelvis performed at initial staging.

---

BONE SCAN FINDINGS:

Technique: Whole-body planar scintigraphy acquired in anterior and posterior projections was performed approximately 3 hours following intravenous administration of 25.7 mCi of Tc-99m MDP. Supplemental SPECT/CT of the pelvis, lumbar spine, and thoracic spine was obtained for lesion characterization and anatomic correlation.

Comparison: Prior whole-body bone scan obtained at time of initial staging.

Overall Impression of Osseous Disease Activity:
There is substantial and unambiguous improvement in the extent and intensity of radiotracer uptake throughout the skeleton when compared to the prior bone scan. This represents a markedly favorable scintigraphic response to systemic therapy.

Region-by-Region Assessment:

Cervical spine and skull base: No abnormal foci of increased radiotracer uptake. No change from prior.

Thoracic spine: The T9 vertebral body, which demonstrated a moderately intense focus of increased uptake on the prior study, now shows only faint, residual increased activity. The degree of uptake is substantially diminished and approaches background. No new thoracic vertebral lesions.

Lumbar spine: The L2 vertebral body lesion previously demonstrated moderately intense uptake. On the current study, this focus persists but with clearly reduced intensity. SPECT/CT correlation localizes residual activity to the right lateral aspect of the L2 vertebral body; the lesion corresponds to a sclerotic focus on the low-dose CT component measuring approximately 1.4 cm, consistent with a healing/sclerotic response to treatment rather than active flare. No new lumbar lesions.

Ribs: The right 4th rib focus of increased uptake previously identified at the posterior-lateral costochondral junction is no longer clearly discernible on the current study, with activity now at background levels. Similarly, the left 6th and 7th rib lesions, which were discrete and clearly visible on the prior scan, show markedly diminished uptake and are now barely perceptible above background. These findings are consistent with a robust therapeutic response at these sites.

Pelvis and hip girdle: The left iliac wing lesion, previously one of the more prominent foci, demonstrates substantially decreased radiotracer activity. The right acetabular lesion shows mildly decreased uptake compared to prior but retains some focal, mild-to-moderate increased activity on SPECT/CT images. The right femoral neck lesion similarly persists with mildly decreased intensity. Residual uptake at these two sites is interpreted as partial response, with viable but less metabolically active osseous disease.

Sternum, clavicles, scapulae: No abnormal radiotracer uptake. No change from prior.

Upper and lower extremities (beyond the hip): No abnormal foci of radiotracer uptake identified in the visualized long bones, knees, or ankles.

Summary of Bone Scan Findings: Of the 8 previously documented osseous metastatic lesions, 4 sites (right 4th rib, left 6th rib, left 7th rib, left iliac wing) show marked reduction in radiotracer uptake consistent with an excellent scintigraphic response. The remaining 4 sites (L2 vertebral body, T9 vertebral body, right acetabulum, right femoral neck) demonstrate reduced but persistent uptake, consistent with partial response. No new osseous lesions are identified. There is no superscan pattern. Background soft tissue activity is normal.

---

CT ABDOMEN AND PELVIS (WITH CONTRAST) FINDINGS:

Technique: Helical acquisition of the abdomen and pelvis following administration of 120 mL of intravenous iohexol (Omnipaque 350) with portal venous phase imaging. Oral contrast administered. Coronal and sagittal reformations reviewed.

Comparison: Prior CT chest/abdomen/pelvis at initial staging.

Note: CT of the chest was not performed on this examination per the ordering protocol. Dedicated chest imaging may be obtained separately if clinically indicated.

Liver: The liver is normal in size and morphology. No focal hepatic lesions are identified. There is no evidence of biliary ductal dilatation. Hepatic parenchyma demonstrates homogeneous enhancement without discrete hypodense or hypervascular nodules.

Spleen: Normal in size at 10.2 cm in long axis. No focal splenic lesions.

Pancreas: Normal morphology without ductal dilatation or focal mass. No peripancreatic stranding.

Adrenal glands: Bilateral adrenal glands are normal in size and morphology. The right adrenal gland measures up to 5 mm in limb width. The left adrenal gland is similarly within normal limits. No adrenal adenoma or metastatic lesion identified. Note: patient is currently on abiraterone acetate; adrenal morphology is unremarkable.

Kidneys: The right kidney measures 10.8 cm and the left kidney measures 11.1 cm in long axis. There is mild bilateral cortical thinning consistent with the patient's known chronic kidney disease. No hydronephrosis. No renal masses. No nephrolithiasis. A 3 mm simple-appearing cyst in the upper pole of the right kidney, unchanged.

Urinary bladder: The bladder is partially distended and demonstrates normal wall thickness without focal lesion.

Lymph nodes - Pelvic:
The bilateral pelvic lymphadenopathy noted at initial staging has regressed substantially.
- Right obturator lymph node: previously measured 1.8 cm in short axis; now measures 0.8 cm. This represents a marked decrease in nodal size, consistent with a strong treatment response.
- Left external iliac lymph node: previously 1.4 cm in short axis; now measures 0.6 cm. Markedly diminished.
- Right external iliac lymph node: previously 1.2 cm; now measures 0.7 cm. Substantially reduced.

Lymph nodes - Retroperitoneal:
The common iliac nodes previously noted at borderline size (up to 1.1 cm) are no longer clearly pathologically enlarged; the largest now measures approximately 0.7 cm in short axis, below threshold for pathologic enlargement. No new retroperitoneal lymphadenopathy is identified.

Lymph nodes - Inguinal: No pathologically enlarged inguinal lymph nodes bilaterally.

Prostate and seminal vesicles:
The prostate gland remains enlarged, estimated volume 46-48 cc, stable compared to the prior examination. There are no striking changes in glandular morphology on CT. Extraprostatic soft tissue irregularity along the posterior margin is again noted, corresponding to known extraprostatic extension on prior MRI, without appreciable change. Bilateral seminal vesicle abnormality (thickening and loss of normal fat plane) appears stable, consistent with known involvement. No new pelvic soft tissue masses or implants are identified.

Bowel: Small and large bowel loops are within normal limits. No obstruction. No acute inflammatory change. Appendix is normal.

Vessels: Aorta and iliac vessels are patent without aneurysm or significant stenosis. Mild atherosclerotic calcification of the infrarenal aorta and iliac vessels is noted, consistent with age and vascular risk factors (hypertension, diabetes).

Soft tissues and musculature: No ascites. No free air. No suspicious soft tissue masses in the pelvic or abdominal wall. Mild subcutaneous edema in the lower abdominal wall and bilateral flanks, which may reflect dependent edema or a medication effect (consistent with the reported lower extremity edema on abiraterone/prednisone).

Osseous structures (CT): On the non-dedicated bone windows:
- L2 vertebral body: a sclerotic focus in the right lateral aspect, as noted on SPECT/CT, is consistent with an evolving sclerotic response at the known metastatic site. No cortical destruction. No height loss.
- Iliac wings: the left iliac wing demonstrates subtle residual sclerosis at the site of prior lytic/mixed lesion, suggesting interval sclerotic healing.
- Right acetabulum and femoral neck: residual subtle sclerosis identified, consistent with previously known lesions in the setting of treatment. No pathologic fracture. Cortical integrity maintained.
- No new lytic or blastic osseous lesions identified on CT of the abdomen and pelvis.

---

IMPRESSION:

1. Markedly favorable scintigraphic and morphologic treatment response to androgen deprivation therapy plus abiraterone acetate, compared to the prior staging bone scan and CT.

2. On bone scan, 4 of 8 previously documented osseous metastatic foci (right 4th rib, left 6th and 7th ribs, left iliac wing) show near-complete resolution of increased tracer uptake. The remaining 4 lesions (L2 vertebral body, T9 vertebral body, right acetabulum, right femoral neck) demonstrate reduced but persistent activity, reflecting partial response. No new osseous lesions.

3. Substantial regression of pelvic lymphadenopathy. The right obturator node has decreased from 1.8 cm to 0.8 cm, the left external iliac node from 1.4 cm to 0.6 cm, and the right external iliac node from 1.2 cm to 0.7 cm. Retroperitoneal nodal disease is no longer clearly enlarged.

4. No new visceral lesions in the liver, adrenal glands, or kidneys. Prostate and seminal vesicle changes are stable, consistent with known local disease and prior treatment effect.

5. Mild subcutaneous/dependent edema noted, which may correlate with reported lower extremity edema in the clinical setting of corticosteroid use.

6. Stable findings of chronic kidney disease on CT (mild bilateral cortical thinning) without hydronephrosis or new parenchymal lesions.

Overall, the imaging findings are consistent with a substantial and widespread treatment response. Residual osseous and lymph nodal disease is present at reduced burden.
